hero.label: UX Research · Automotive & Digital Product
hero.name: Yeonjune Kim
hero.tagline: I translate user language into product language.
hero.summary:
I studied Psychology and Human-Computer Interaction (HCI), and I've been working as a UX researcher for five years.
Most of my work has been with automotive manufacturers, major consumer electronics, and tech companies.
The best part of my job is when what I learned from users actually makes it into the product.

projects.title: PBV Digital UX Feature Validation Study
projects.description: A global study verifying the proposed UX features of a major Korean OEM's PBV (Purpose-Built Vehicle) in a VR environment with commercial vehicle drivers in Korea and Germany. Contributed to deriving improvement recommendations by differentiating between cargo van and passenger van drivers.

detail.hero: A global study verifying the proposed UX features of a major Korean OEM's PBV (Purpose-Built Vehicle) in a VR environment with commercial vehicle drivers in Korea and Germany. Contributed to deriving improvement recommendations by differentiating between cargo van and passenger van drivers.

overview.background: A major Korean automotive OEM was reviewing specialized UX specifications for commercial vehicles as part of its PBV (Purpose-Built Vehicle) development. Prior to physical vehicle development, the team needed to validate the operational logic of proposed UX features using VR and identify areas for improvement early, while also accounting for differences between the Korean and European (German) commercial vehicle markets.
goal: Validate the operational logic of proposed PBV UX specifications and specialized features
Derive feature priorities (High / Mid / Low) aligned with the work contexts of cargo van and passenger van drivers
role: Usability test survey and in-depth interview guideline development

research.intro: We designed a scenario-based usability test using a VR environment to let participants experience each UX feature in real work scenarios. Cargo (Delivery/Worker) and passenger van drivers were recruited separately to reflect differences in work context, with a focus on uncovering additional scenarios applicable to actual job tasks.

methods.name: VR-Based Scenario Usability Testing
methods.description: The same VR setup was deployed in Seoul (Korea) and Germany, allowing participants to experience the vehicle's proposed UX features within realistic work scenarios. Usefulness and satisfaction were rated on a 7-point scale for each feature.
methods.count: Approx. 30 in Korea / Approx. 30 in Germany

methods.name: In-Depth Interviews
methods.description: Interviews were conducted with each participant immediately after the VR session to explore qualitative reactions to each feature and potential usage scenarios in real work contexts. Additional user needs corresponding to requests from the client's development and engineering team were also collected.

findings.title: Feature priority structures are clearly separated between cargo and passenger van drivers
findings.description: Cargo drivers evaluated features primarily around operational efficiency and safety, while passenger van drivers placed higher value on space utilization and passenger comfort. The work context specific to each vehicle type directly influenced overall feature acceptance.

findings.title: Korean and German drivers evaluated the same features in different ways
findings.description:
Some features received high usefulness ratings from Korean drivers but were rated comparatively lower by German drivers.
Differences in work practices, fleet operation characteristics, and local regulatory environments were identified as the primary factors.

findings.title: Across both markets, drivers wanted customizable features tied to their work related apps or fleet management system
findings.description:
Across customizable feature categories, a consistent need for integration with work apps and platforms was identified.
Flexibility to embed features into existing work flows — rather than standalone functionality — emerged as a key variable in feature acceptance.

outcome.summary: A Korea–Germany comparative priority matrix was derived across multiple proposed UX features, with feature-level improvement insights and recommendations delivered to the client.

projects.title: New Product Opportunity Study for the US Automotive Parts Market
projects.description: An exploratory consumer study to identify new product opportunity areas for a global automotive parts manufacturer entering the US market — conducted on-site in LA and Detroit.

overview.background: The client, a global automotive parts manufacturer, was exploring new product directions for the US market, leveraging its automotive manufacturing expertise. An early-stage understanding of American consumers' vehicle usage contexts, unmet needs, and product opportunity areas was needed before committing to a development direction.
goal: Understand US consumers' vehicle usage patterns and unmet needs related to existing product categories
Identify latent needs through in-context observation and surface new product opportunity areas

research.intro: Working with a US-based local research partner, we conducted multi-method fieldwork across LA and Detroit. The two cities were strategically selected to capture contrasting climate, infrastructure, and cultural contexts — yielding a richer range of consumer insights.

methods.name: Focus Group Discussion (FGD)
methods.description: Group discussions in LA and Detroit, segmented by vehicle type and buyer profile. Explored unmet needs with existing products and reactions to unapplied features.

methods.name: Extreme User IDI + Drive Along
methods.description: In-home visits (Home Tour + Car Tour) followed by ride-along observation to capture vehicle usage behavior in real-life context. Surfaced unarticulated pain points and latent needs.

methods.name: Semi-Professional In-Depth Interviews
methods.description: Online interviews with automotive enthusiasts and industry-adjacent participants to capture expert-level perspectives not accessible through general consumer research.

findings.title: Product acceptance expands from functional reliability to emotional value — but only in that order
findings.description: For electrified components, mass-market consumers prioritized operational reliability above all else, while premium and EV segment users showed clear interest in design aesthetics and personalization — but only when basic reliability was assumed. Emotional appeals backfired when reliability concerns hadn't been addressed, amplifying rather than offsetting worry about malfunctions.

findings.title: LA and Detroit wanted different things
findings.description: Detroit consumers selected cold-weather durability and freeze-resistance as core requirements, while LA consumers showed stronger interest in EV-compatible design and premium experiential features. The same product requires different core value propositions depending on the target region.

findings.title: Personalization experience emerges as the core opportunity area in EV and premium segments
findings.description: Strong demand for personalized in-car experiences — welcome sequences, driver-responsive lighting and sound — was concentrated among EV and premium vehicle users. Products positioned as active, context-aware agents rather than passive components represent the most differentiated long-term opportunity.

outcome.summary: Vehicle usage context, product-level unmet needs, and new product opportunity areas were synthesized and delivered to the client as a foundation for their US market product strategy.

projects.title: European Feature Value Study
projects.description: A quantitative and qualitative study measuring European consumer acceptance and perceived value of future vehicle features across 10 vehicle segments. Used KANO analysis and price elasticity modeling to identify optimal value ranges and feature adoption strategies.

overview.background: A major Korean automotive OEM conducted a comprehensive feature value study spanning 10 vehicle segments, accounting for the fact that perceived spec value differs by vehicle class.
goal: Deliver optimal value ranges and feature adoption strategies using Kano analysis and the Price Sensitivity Meter (PSM)
role: Quantitative gang survey and Mini-FGD management (in collaboration with local research firm)

research.intro: A quantitative gang survey measured feature acceptance and perceived value at scale, while Mini-FGDs explored real-life usage contexts and scenarios. Kano analysis was applied to classify feature characteristics.

methods.name: Quantitative Gang Survey
methods.description:
European primary drivers aged 20–50 who purchased a vehicle in the past 2–3 years, equal quotas per segment across 10 segments.
Survey comprised: standalone feature evaluation, Kano analysis, unaided price expectation, and a 4-step acceptance threshold measurement.

methods.name: Focus Group Discussion (FGD)
methods.description: 00 group FGD sessions representing 10 vehicle segments — exploring real-life feature usage contexts, value perception gaps arising from limited prior experience, and the presence of low-cost substitutes.

findings.title: Most future features are perceived as KANO 'attractive' — nice-to-have, not expected
findings.description:
The majority of evaluated features were classified as attractive specs —
features whose absence is acceptable but whose presence generates delight. Connectivity, infotainment, and convenience features were additionally recognized as higher-necessity specs.

findings.title: Design features feel unnecessary — until the price comes in lower than expected
findings.description: Design-oriented features are rated low on necessity, but when the presented price falls below consumer expectations, acceptance forms quickly and adoption rates rise substantially.

findings.title: Consumers undervalue features they haven't tried — especially when a cheap alternative already exists
findings.description: Features that consumers have never experienced before, or can already replicate with a low-cost workaround, consistently receive low value ratings. Price reductions or hands-on trial opportunities are needed to shift perception.

outcome.summary:
Delivered a feature value matrix covering Kano classification, optimal value ranges, and the price threshold required for target adoption per feature category.
Findings directly supported the client's internal decision-making on European feature adoption.

projects.title: D-Segment Ethnography
projects.client: Global Premium Automotive Brand (via Global Research Agency)
projects.description:
An in-depth ethnographic study with luxury D-segment vehicle owners in Korea (G90, BMW X7, Range Rover, A8, etc.) exploring lifestyle, vehicle and brand perception, design preferences, and next-vehicle needs.
Conducted locally under a Global Research Agency framework.

overview.background:
A global premium automotive OEM commissioned market-specific ethnographic research across key markets to assess its global D-segment competitiveness. In South Korea, the goal was to deeply explore how owners — across premium
automotive brands — articulate their lifestyle, vehicle purchase journey, interior aesthetic preferences, and expectations for future vehicles, to surface implications for brand strategy.
goal: Deeply explore the lifestyle and symbolic meanings D-segment owners in Korea attach to their vehicles
Assess design and emerging technology preferences to deliver Korean market implications for brand positioning improvement
role: Study design (applying Global Research Agency framework)

research.intro:
Applied the Global Research Agency's ethnography methodology, conducting in-depth home visit interviews with each participant.
Sessions took place inside respondents' homes and vehicles, covering a broad range of topics from lifestyle exploration to future vehicle needs.

methods.name: In-Depth Ethnographic Interviews
methods.description:
Home and vehicle visits with D-segment owners, observing lifestyle, living environment, and usage patterns.
Participants were recruited by vehicle type (sedan/SUV) and brand.

methods.name: Moderator Observation Notes & Diary Collection
methods.description: Participant-level observation notes and diary-style photo documentation were compiled for each session and recorded in the data platform.

findings.title: The D-segment vehicle is both a social signal and a source of self-efficacy
findings.description: In Korea's 'projected self' culture, a D-segment vehicle is the most visible marker of socioeconomic status. Sedan owners project dignity and social authority, while SUV owners project dynamism and versatility — both dimensions are central to why owners chose their vehicle.

findings.title: Luxury is defined by material quality and finish, not digital features
findings.description:
D-Segment owners assess the level of luxury through sensory details — material texture, stitching precision, and material consistency throughout the interior.
Rather than display size or count, it is the harmony of the spatial layout and the completeness of the finish that determines whether a vehicle reads as truly premium.

findings.title: The client earns loyalty for design consistency but is losing ground to competing OEMs
findings.description: Brand owners value its restrained design and driving quality, but feel the brand is undervalued in Korea due to pricing strategy and a diminishing marketing presence. Non-owners perceive the brand as falling behind competing OEMs on performance, comfort, and design innovation.

outcome.summary:
Ethnographic insights from 00 Korean D-segment owners were reported to a global premium automotive brand.
Findings on Korean consumers' brand perception, lifestyle, and future vehicle expectations were compiled into implications for positioning improvement.

items.label: Brands Covered
items.value:
BMW, Audi,
Mercedes-Benz, and other
premium automotive brands

items.label: Deliverables
items.value:
Analysis report (.pptx)
Interview video with English subtitles

projects.title: IoT Platform App Widget & AI Concept Validation Study
projects.client: Major Korean Consumer Electronics & IT Company
projects.description: A study validating user responses to a new widget concept and AI suggestion feature in a client's IoT platform app. Combined widget placement tasks with AI concept evaluation.

overview.background: The client was evaluating the introduction of a new widget UI concept and AI suggestion features for their IoT platform app, and needed to understand real user acceptance and concerns in advance. The study also explored U&A of the existing app interface to validate how the new concepts could improve upon it. A client-provided prototype was installed on dedicated test phones prepared for the sessions.
goal: Understand user acceptance of the new widget concept and preferred placement patterns
Validate user responses and concerns across various AI suggestion concept types
role: Study setup (UT cam layout configuration and video editing)

research.intro: After setting up test phones pre-loaded with a client-provided prototype, the study proceeded sequentially through current app U&A exploration and new concept response collection. Participants were recruited in three groups (high / mid / low) based on the number of connected devices at home to reflect differences in usage patterns.

methods.name: In-Depth Interviews (IDI)
methods.description: 1:1 in-depth sessions covering current app U&A exploration, reactions after viewing the new widget concept video, and responses to AI suggestion concepts.

methods.name: Widget Placement Task
methods.description: An individual task using paper prototypes, where participants arranged their own widget home screen layout. Explored placement preferences and priority criteria in real usage contexts.

findings.title: The widget concept is positively received in terms of device control efficiency and design
findings.description: Reducing the additional depth previously required for detailed control was the key acceptance factor. The richer color palette and improved information readability were also rated positively.

findings.title: AI suggestions are accepted when aligned with the user's current intent and context, but trigger rejection when they interfere with control goals
findings.description: Users enter the app with a clear intent to control devices, and suggestions that interrupt this flow generated strong negative reactions. Expectations for AI assistance were concentrated in routine and automation setup rather than device control itself.

findings.title: Widget placement decisions are driven by control frequency and the need for detailed interaction
findings.description: A consistent pattern emerged of placing frequently used, detail-heavy devices at the top and right side of the screen. Users strongly preferred fitting all widgets within a single page, prioritizing control widgets over monitoring ones.

outcome.summary: User responses and widget placement preferences regarding the widget concept and AI suggestion feature were compiled and delivered to the client. A research report covering acceptance levels and concerns by concept type was provided.

projects.title: Global MMORPG Expansion Pack Positioning & Messaging Study
projects.client: Global Gaming Company (via Global Research Agency)
projects.description: A qualitative study validating the positioning and messaging strategy for a major MMORPG's upcoming expansion. Separate FGD groups for active players, lapsed players, and non-players explored message receptiveness and return/acquisition potential.

overview.background: A global game publisher needed to validate ahead of a new MMORPG expansion launch how its positioning strategy and key messages would land across target user segments. Participated as the Korean market partner in collaboration with a global research agency.
goal: Assess how the expansion's positioning and core messages land across active, lapsed, and potential player segments
Identify new content reactions and derive segment-level communication strategy directions

research.intro:
Three player segments (Active, Lapsed, Non-player Potential) and two age bands (18–35, 35–49)
were recruited separately and grouped into FGDs.

methods.name: Focus Group Discussion (FGD)
methods.description: Three player segments (Active, Lapsed, Non-player Potential) and two age bands (18–35, 35–49) were recruited separately and grouped into FGDs.

findings.title: Active players respond to lore continuity; lapsed players need a concrete reason to return
findings.description: Current active players reacted positively to narrative and world-building continuity. Lapsed players, by contrast, needed the messaging to clearly answer 'what has changed since I left' — without that, the expansion felt like more of the same rather than a meaningful re-entry point.

findings.title: For potential players, years of accumulated content feel like a barrier, not a feature
findings.description: The long-running nature of the game — while a strength for 'veterans' — created a psychological entry barrier for non-players. Messaging that clearly positions the expansion as accessible to first-time players substantially increased openness to trying the game.

findings.title: 18–35 and 35–49 players have distinct motivations and engagement styles
findings.description: The 18–35 group wanted new content and an active community to dive into. The 35–49 group compared nostalgia for the game's past against how much time it now demands — for them, whether the game is worth the hours is the key question.

outcome.summary: Delivered segment-level message receptiveness findings and communication direction recommendations to the global research agency, with a dedicated Korean market implications section highlighting local nuances.

projects.title: Korean Apartment Interior Trend Exploration
projects.description: An exploratory expert interview study examining shifts in Korean apartment interior trends — capturing their perspectives on evolving consumer needs, emerging styles, and material preferences to inform product and service development.

overview.background: As South Korea's apartment interior market matures, consumer preferences are fragmenting and upgrading. The client needed an early read on the latest interior trends and their underlying drivers — from industry practitioners with direct market exposure — to feed into product and service planning.
goal: Explore the current direction of Korean apartment interior trends and changing consumer needs through practitioner perspectives
Derive actionable implications for the client's product and service strategy

research.intro: We prioritized industry expert insight over direct consumer research — an exploratory approach appropriate for rapidly evolving trend topics where experts observe consumer behavior patterns in aggregate. Interior designers, construction industry experts, and trend specialists were targeted.

methods.name: Expert In-Depth Interviews (IDI)
methods.description: 1:1 in-depth interviews with 3 apartment interior industry experts — exploring current consumer demand shifts, emerging styles and materials, and the direction the market is heading.

findings.title: Expanded community facilities are redefining kitchens and living rooms as spaces for social display — reducing demand for in-unit cooking and study space
findings.description: As apartment complexes add cafeterias, study rooms, and fitness centers, residents' need for in-unit cooking, storage, and home offices is declining. In their place, kitchens and living rooms are becoming social and showcase spaces — driving demand for wide dining tables, open-plan layouts, and visually polished interiors.

findings.title: Floor plan trends are shifting from 'maximize room numbers' (4th gen) to 'maximize openness and views' (5th gen)
findings.description: The dominant layout is moving from four bedrooms, U-shaped kitchens, and maximum in-unit storage toward three bedrooms, wide 11-line dining areas, and view-facing fixed windows. Dedicated storage units, pantries, and entry closets now handle storage outside the main living space, freeing it for openness.

findings.title: Rising design literacy and smart convenience demands are emerging as the new competitive battleground in residential development
findings.description: Post-pandemic exposure to interior design via social media and TV has raised consumer standards — fueling demand for personalized packages and imported furniture. Simultaneously, app-controlled community management, last-mile delivery robots, two-car-per-household parking design, and wider window openings are becoming must-have differentiators in new residential launches.

outcome.summary: Expert-sourced apartment interior trend findings provided the client with an industry-view baseline for product and service planning decisions.

projects.title: Voice Agent Speech Input Analysis & VUX Evaluation
projects.client: Major Korean Consumer Electronics & IT Company
projects.description:
A VUX evaluation study assessing the interactions of two voice agents embedded in the company's smartphones.
Identified 00 UX issues across 00 categories with improvement directions.

overview.background:
Participated in a project evaluating the English utterance interactions of two voice agents embedded in the company’s smartphones.
The core task was to systematically identify UX issues in English-language usage and derive improvement directions.
goal: Classify voice agent interaction issues in English utterance environments by type and pattern
Derive prioritized improvement directions for identified issues

research.intro:
Analyzed the voice agent interaction flow step by step using Korean and English utterances,
identifying mismatch patterns between intended commands and actual system responses.

methods.name: Korean/English Speech Input Testing
methods.description:
Conducted utterance scenario testing across multiple environments including first-party and competitor devices.
Logged mismatches between intended commands and actual system responses to surface recurring patterns.

methods.name: Issue Classification & Typology
methods.description:
Organized discovered issues by interaction flow stage and derived recurring patterns.
Sorted by occurrence frequency and user impact to establish improvement priorities.

findings.title: Background noise and speech characteristics undermine wake word reliability
findings.description: Loud music, video playback, and timer alarms all cause wake word recognition failure. Pauses during speech and low or dropping vocal pitch also trigger premature input termination. The voice agent lacks sufficient robustness for the noisy, varied environments of real daily use.

findings.title: Context over-application and under-application co-exist, eroding user trust
findings.description: The voice agent both over-applies prior utterance context (acting on outdated intent) and fails to apply it when users expect continuity. The root cause is that context is determined by system-internal state (input field activation) rather than user behavioral context — a misaligned design assumption.

findings.title: Device control commands are frequently misrouted to internet search
findings.description: Requests to control smart home devices — TV playback, air purifier mode changes, lighting — were frequently mishandled as internet searches. The pattern reveals that the voice agent struggles to reliably distinguish between device-control intent and information-retrieval intent when commands are phrased naturally.

outcome.summary: Identified and organized key UX issues in the English utterance environment, with improvement directions for each.

projects.title: MI Insights Forum Operations ('22–'23)
projects.description:
A two-year market intelligence forum analyzing SDV transition, EV strategy, and emerging trends in the global automotive market.
In 2022, covered FoD and EV strategies across four regions (US, Europe, India, China); in 2023, analyzed EV, SDV, and infotainment strategies
across five regions (US, Europe, ASEAN, China, India).

overview.background: The client needed a comprehensive view of FoD subscription service dynamics and EV market evolution across major global markets (2022), followed in 2023 by a structured analysis of EV, SDV, and infotainment strategies across five regions — US, Europe, ASEAN, China, and India — with a focus on how emerging players were outpacing traditional OEMs in software-defined vehicle innovation.
goal: 2022: Intelligence gathering across four regions — US (FoD strategy), Europe (EV pricing structure), India (EV policy & infrastructure), China (EV brands & channels)
2023: Analysis of EV, SDV, and infotainment strategies across five regions — US, Europe, ASEAN, China, and India

research.intro: Collaborated with regional research partners for recruiting and operations. For 2023, worked with local experts across five regions (US, Europe, ASEAN, China, India) to collect and analyze primary sources (company presentations, product reviews) and secondary sources (market reports, policy documents).

methods.name: [2022] Four-Region Research
methods.description: Conducted regional research with local partners: US (Tesla FoD strategy + consumer survey), Europe (EV mid-to-long-term pricing desk research + expert interviews), India (EV policy & infrastructure trends), China (EV brand & channel case analysis).

methods.name: [2023] Five-Region Research
methods.description: US & Europe (EV/SDV strategy), ASEAN (production base utilization and FTA strategy), China (infotainment R&D types, HMI trends, SDV transition cases), India (powertrain strategy) — conducted with regional experts and local partners.

findings.title: FoD only succeeds when focused on value consumers can experience immediately
findings.description: Tesla's FoD strategy concentrates on features with instantly perceptible value. Subscription models built around features that consumers don't recognize as valuable face strong resistance.

findings.title: Self-development strategy offers the highest chance of infotainment innovation success
findings.description: Traditional supplier-dependent approaches produce poor results due to cross-domain coordination failures, while OEM+IT joint ventures suffer from internal conflicts that undermine longevity. Strategies like Li Xiang and NIO — which vertically integrate while owning the full experience definition — are best positioned to deliver differentiated products.

findings.title: Proactive response to region-specific regulatory timelines is essential for EV strategy
findings.description: India's BS7 transition and the uneven pace of EV adoption across European markets demand region-specific approaches rather than a single global strategy. Proactive positioning aligned with policy timelines is the key variable in capturing market share.

outcome.summary: Planned and delivered the MI Insights Forum across two consecutive years, synthesizing global EV and SDV market intelligence into trilingual presentation materials (Korean, English, Chinese) for the client's internal strategic planning.

projects.title: Next-Gen Light Commercial Truck Product Competitiveness Study
projects.description:
A consumer product competitiveness study for a next-generation light commercial truck, covering exterior, interior, features, and trim.
Combined a gang survey and FGDs to map consumer preferences and purchase intent across electrification and LPDi powertrain options.

overview.background: South Korea's light commercial truck market faces sustained demand decline due to an aging workforce. The client needed to assess consumer product perceptions and feature preferences ahead of the next-generation model's launch to guide specification decisions.
goal: Assess the next-generation light commercial truck across exterior/interior design competitiveness, feature necessity and preference, trim composition appropriateness, and consumer response
Reflect findings in launch strategy and specification prioritization decisions

research.intro: We designed a mixed-method structure: quantitative survey to establish directional findings at scale, followed by FGDs to excavate the contextual 'why' behind the numbers. Powertrain preference groups (LPDi vs. EV) were analyzed separately to capture divergent usage patterns.

methods.name: Gang Survey
methods.description: A development-stage vehicle buck and the current model were displayed side by side on-site. Current light truck owners and purchase-intenders directly compared and evaluated both vehicles across exterior design, interior, features, trim, and powertrain.

methods.name: Focus Group Discussion (FGD)
methods.description: Separate groups for LPDi and EV preference segments explored purchase rationale, real-world feature usage context, and reactions to trim configurations in depth.

findings.title: The next-gen truck earns overwhelming design preference by breaking the 'workhorse' mold
findings.description: An exterior design language that conveys both refinement and toughness emerged as the primary preference driver. Exterior elements that improve practicality and a clean aesthetic were especially well-received.

findings.title: LPDi perception is improving but still carries legacy skepticism
findings.description: Negative preconceptions around LPDi have not fully dissolved, but actual users tend to report satisfaction with engine performance. EV adoption faces strong range anxiety — even for primarily urban delivery use, consumers expressed significant concern about AER adequacy.

findings.title: Safety features and driving convenience options dominate preference rankings
findings.description: Safety-enhancing features and driving convenience options dominated the preference rankings. Improved nighttime visibility and enhanced crash safety were the primary rationales behind these choices.

outcome.summary: Delivered a comprehensive product competitiveness diagnostic across all dimensions — exterior, interior, features, trim, and powertrain — directly contributing to launch strategy and specification prioritization decisions.

items.label: Powertrain coverage
items.value: LPDi and EV analyzed in parallel

projects.title: Automotive OEM C-SUV EV Positioning Clinic
projects.description:
A global car clinic validating the product competitiveness and positioning strategy of a C-SUV EV.
Explored design, USP, brand value, and consumer segmentation with consumers in Germany and China.

overview.background: As competition in China's EV market intensified with the rise of local brands like NIO, Xpeng, and BYD, a major Korean automotive OEM needed to validate how its C-SUV EV's design, USPs, and positioning landed with actual target consumers. Running simultaneous car clinics in China and Germany was the core task — to capture and compare market-level differences in consumer response.
goal: Validate the vehicle's exterior design preference, core USP market viability, brand premium perception, and consumer segmentation
Refine the global positioning strategy through simultaneous Germany and China market comparison

research.intro: Applied a car clinic methodology using 3D vehicle imagery (VRED files) to explore consumer reactions. Conducted in Shanghai (China) and Frankfurt (Germany) to enable cross-market comparative analysis.

methods.name: Positioning Clinic (Gang Survey)
methods.description: Explored the C-SUV EV's design, competitive positioning, USP validity, and feature preferences through a car clinic format.

methods.name: Focus Group Discussion (FGD)
methods.description: Conducted small-group discussions with selected participants from the gang survey to explore the context and perceptions behind the quantitative findings.

methods.name: Consumer Photo Diary + IDI
methods.description: Collected consumers' lifestyle and vehicle usage experiences through a pre-study photo diary, followed by individual in-depth interviews.

findings.title: Lower EV brand recognition versus competitors limited price premium acceptance in both markets
findings.description:
In both markets, the OEM's EV brand recognition lagged significantly behind competitors,
directly suppressing consumer willingness to pay a price premium for the vehicle.

findings.title: The bold, distinctive exterior design appeals to self-expressive buyers — but limits mainstream positioning reach
findings.description:
The bold, distinctive exterior design drew strong positive responses from self-expressive consumer segments,
but was perceived as unfamiliar by mainstream consumers — limiting its positioning reach.

findings.title: Long-range driving capability and interior spaciousness were validated as the model's core USPs across both markets
findings.description:
Across both markets, long-range AER and interior spaciousness emerged as the top EV purchase drivers,
validating these as the vehicle's strongest and most defensible USPs.

outcome.summary: Conducted car clinics across Germany and China to define the core USP communication direction and global positioning strategy for the client's C-SUV EV.

projects.title: Brazil C-SUV Market & Consumer Research
projects.description:
A mixed-methods study to understand the growth trajectory and consumer landscape of Brazil's C-SUV market.
Combining desk research, expert IDIs, consumer FGDs, and quantitative survey to define target consumer segments
and strategic implications for the client.

overview.background: Brazil's C-SUV market was growing on a foundation of affluent, aspirational consumers, with further expansion expected as the price gap with smaller segments narrowed. The client needed a deep understanding of local consumer dynamics to reposition its C-SUV model and plan its broader product strategy in Brazil.
goal: Identify the growth drivers and consumer characteristics of Brazil's C-SUV market and develop a purchase-motivation-based consumer segmentation
Deliver product and marketing strategy direction for the client

research.intro:
The study was structured to link existing NCBS (New Car Buyer Survey) quantitative data with the new research for integrated analysis.
Expert IDIs established the market hypothesis; consumer FGDs and a quantitative survey refined and validated it.

methods.name: Expert In-Depth Interviews (IDI)
methods.description: Brazilian automotive industry experts based in São Paulo provided contextual grounding on C-SUV market dynamics, consumer characteristics, and market trends — establishing hypotheses for the main consumer study.

methods.name: Focus Group Discussion (FGD)
methods.description:
C-SUV owners and purchase intenders explored vehicle purchase motivation, brand perceptions, feature needs, and future outlook.
A separate group of the client's C-SUV model owners was run to capture incumbent user perspective.

methods.name: Quantitative Survey (NCBS-linked)
methods.description: Integrated analysis with existing NCBS quantitative data validated the directionality and scale of the consumer segmentation emerging from qualitative phases.

findings.title: The C-SUV is both a status symbol and a practical choice for Brazil's road realities
findings.description:
Brazilian consumers view C-SUVs as visible markers of social status.
Simultaneously, they are a practical response to Brazil's challenging road conditions — providing safety and comfort for family travel.

findings.title: The client's C-SUV model is trusted as 'young and reliable' — but reads as outdated
findings.description: The client lacks brand differentiation versus key competitors. Its C-SUV model, once a pioneer in the segment, is now perceived as dated. Repositioning through new technology and design updates is needed.

findings.title: Consumer motivation and attitude produce distinct segments
findings.description: Based on purchase motivation and lifestyle orientation, distinct consumer segments were identified — each with different product attribute requirements and communication needs.

outcome.summary: Delivered a report covering Brazil's C-SUV market growth diagnosis, consumer segmentation findings, and the client's C-SUV model repositioning direction.

projects.title: Global Consumer Goods Company Mosquito Repellent Online HUT
projects.client: Global Consumer Goods Company (via Global Research Agency)
projects.description: An online Home Use Test (HUT) with Korean consumers evaluating Global Consumer Goods Company's mosquito repellent product line — exploring real-world usage experience, performance perception, usage context, and improvement directions as part of a Global Research Agency study.

overview.background: Global Consumer Goods Company sought to validate the Korean market fit of its mosquito repellent product lineup, capturing real consumer usage experiences and product performance perceptions. The study was conducted as a local partner study within the Global Research Agency research network.
goal: Explore real-world usage experiences with mosquito repellent products — capturing performance perception, scent preference, ease of use, and satisfaction
Deliver improvement directions and Korean market implications
role: HUT design and online survey management

research.intro:
Applied the global research agency's HUT methodology framework — designing an online study where Korean consumers used the product in their real home environment
before reporting their experience through an online survey.

methods.name: Online HUT (Home Use Test)
methods.description: Products were sent to participants for in-home use over a defined period. Online surveys captured usage experience, performance perception, satisfaction, and improvement suggestions — all reported from real-life context.

findings.title: Online HUT — Capturing Real-Use Context
findings.description:
Products were shipped to participants for use during the peak mosquito season (Jul–Sep),
collecting responses from natural home environments rather than lab settings.

findings.title: Recruitment Design — Current Electric Liquid Diffuser Users Only
findings.description:
Recruitment was limited to current users of electric liquid mosquito repellent products,
enabling comparative evaluation from consumers with existing category experience.

findings.title: Global Framework Integration — Local Partner Operations
findings.description:
Participated as the local research partner — collecting and delivering Korean consumer data
in alignment with the global research agency's standard HUT framework.

outcome.summary: Korean consumer usage experience data was integrated into the Global Research Agency framework, contributing to Global Consumer Goods Company's product improvement roadmap and Korean market marketing strategy.

about.bio: With a background in Psychology (BA) and Human-Computer Interaction (PgCert), I have spent 4 years as a UX Researcher translating user data into actionable insights. Most of my work has been with automotive manufacturers, major consumer electronics and tech companies, leading projects across 7 countries — the US, Germany, India, China, and beyond. My strength lies in bridging the gap between users and product teams through rigorous research and clear, bilingual communication.

bullets: Conducted UX research for major automotive OEMs, consumer electronics companies, and fintech platforms
Managed and moderated projects across 7 countries (US, Germany, India, China, Saudi Arabia, and more)
Led 100+ in-depth interviews in Korean and English, synthesizing findings into actionable insights
Designed and executed diverse research methods: Home Visits, Shadowing, VR-based scenario testing
Produced bilingual (Korean/English) research reports and video deliverables

experience.org: Republic of Korea Army, 1st Artillery Brigade
bullets: Analyzed military intelligence and managed classified documentation
Collaborated with higher command units and prepared briefing reports

education.note: Exchange Student — University of Ottawa, Canada (2015 – 2016)
